steps.narration: What if I told you every curve has a hidden speed at every point?
Take this curve, f of x equals x squared. Near the bottom, around x equals zero, it's almost flat. But as you move to the right, toward x equals two or three, it climbs faster and faster.

steps.content: How steep is the curve at ONE specific point?
steps.narration: So the big question is: how steep is this curve at one specific point? Not on average. At exactly that spot.

steps.content: Secant Line: a straight line through two points on the curve
steps.narration: Here's the idea. Pick two points on the curve and draw a straight line through them. That's called a secant line. Its slope gives you the average rate of change between those two points.
The slope of that secant line is f of x plus h minus f of x, all divided by h. That h is the horizontal gap between your two x values.

steps.content: Now slide the second point closer... and closer...
steps.narration: Now here's where it gets beautiful. Slide that second point closer and closer to the first one. In math, we call this taking a limit. As h shrinks toward zero, the secant line rotates and settles into one perfect position.
When h reaches zero, the secant line becomes a tangent line. A line that just touches the curve at that single point, matching its exact steepness.
And that's the derivative. The limit as h approaches zero of f of x plus h minus f of x over h. It gives you the exact slope of the curve at any single point.

steps.content: Derivative = slope of the tangent line = instantaneous rate of change
steps.narration: The derivative is the slope of the tangent line. Another way to say it: the instantaneous rate of change. This is exactly how physics computes velocity from a position curve.

steps.content: Every derivative rule is just a shortcut for this limit.
steps.narration: That's all a derivative is. A slope at a single point. And every derivative rule you'll ever learn? Just a shortcut for computing this limit.
